title: Data & Storage
description: Room database, DataStore, auto-migrations, indexing, WAL mode, and offline-first sync
lesson:
Local Data Persistence

Android offers several storage options, each suited for different use cases. Choosing the right one is critical for performance, UX, and data integrity:

Storage Decision Guide: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Data Type Best Option Why ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Structured / relational Room SQL, compile-time checks Key-value preferences Preferences DS Async, type-safe keys Typed config objects Proto DataStore Full schema via protobuf Large binary files File storage Images, downloads, PDFs Shared media MediaStore Photos, videos, audio Cross-app data ContentProvider URI-based, permissioned Temporary / cache Cache dir Auto-cleaned by system ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  • Room — SQLite abstraction for structured data with compile-time query verification
  • DataStore — Key-value or typed data storage, replacing SharedPreferences
  • File storage — For large files (images, downloads) in app-specific or shared locations
  • ContentProvider — For exposing data to other apps or the system

Room Deep Dive

Room is a persistence library that provides an abstraction layer over SQLite to allow fluent database access while harnessing the full power of SQLite. It has three core components: Entity (defines table schema), DAO (defines queries), and Database (ties them together and holds the connection).

Room Architecture: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Repository / ViewModel │ ▼ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ DAO │──▶│ Database │──▶│ SQLite DB │ │Interface│ │ abstract │ │ (file on │ │ │ │ class │ │ disk) │ └─────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────────┘ ▲ │ @Query/@Insert @TypeConverters @Update/@Delete @AutoMigration │ │ Compile-time Runtime SQL validation type mapping ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Entity — Defining Tables

An @Entity maps a Kotlin data class to a SQLite table. Each property becomes a column. Room validates that every Entity has at least one @PrimaryKey at compile time.

@Entity( tableName = "users", indices = [ Index("email", unique = true), Index("lastName", "firstName") // composite index ] ) data class User( @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0, val email: String, val firstName: String, val lastName: String, @ColumnInfo(name = "created_at") val createdAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), @ColumnInfo(defaultValue = "active") val status: String = "active", @Ignore val tempFlag: Boolean = false // not stored in DB )
Entity annotations summary:
@PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) — auto-incrementing ID
@ColumnInfo(name = "...") — customize column name (decouples DB schema from Kotlin naming)
@ColumnInfo(defaultValue = "...") — SQLite default for migrations adding this column
@Ignore — exclude a field from the database table
@Embedded — flatten a nested object's fields into the parent table

Embedded Objects and Relationships

Use @Embedded to store a nested object as part of the same table (its fields become columns). Use @Relation for one-to-many queries across tables:

// @Embedded flattens Address fields into the users table data class Address( val street: String, val city: String, val zipCode: String ) @Entity(tableName = "users") data class User( @PrimaryKey val id: Long, val name: String, @Embedded(prefix = "addr_") val address: Address // Columns: id, name, addr_street, addr_city, addr_zipCode ) // One-to-many relationship data class UserWithPosts( @Embedded val user: User, @Relation(parentColumn = "id", entityColumn = "authorId") val posts: List<Post> ) // DAO query for relationship @Transaction // ensures atomic read of user + posts @Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :userId") suspend fun getUserWithPosts(userId: Long): UserWithPosts
Always use @Transaction with @Relation queries. Without it, Room runs multiple queries (one for the parent, one for the children) that could see inconsistent data if a write happens between them.

DAO — Data Access Object

DAOs are interfaces or abstract classes annotated with @Dao. Room generates the implementation at compile time and validates all SQL queries against the schema. A DAO method can return:

  • Flow<List<T>> — reactive stream, re-emits whenever the underlying table changes
  • suspend fun — one-shot coroutine, runs on the calling dispatcher (usually IO)
  • LiveData<T> — lifecycle-aware observable (legacy, prefer Flow)
  • PagingSource<Int, T> — for Paging 3 integration with large datasets
@Dao interface UserDao { // Flow return → reactive, auto-updates when data changes @Query("SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY lastName") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<User>> // suspend → one-shot query on background thread @Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :userId") suspend fun getById(userId: Long): User? // LIKE query with parameter binding @Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%' || :query || '%'") fun search(query: String): Flow<List<User>> // Paging 3 integration for large datasets @Query("SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY lastName") fun pagingSource(): PagingSource<Int, User> // Upsert: insert or replace on conflict @Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE) suspend fun upsert(user: User) // Room 2.5+: built-in @Upsert annotation @Upsert suspend fun upsertUsers(users: List<User>) @Insert suspend fun insertAll(users: List<User>) @Update suspend fun update(user: User) @Delete suspend fun delete(user: User) @Query("DELETE FROM users") suspend fun deleteAll() // Return insert IDs for verification @Insert suspend fun insertAndGetId(user: User): Long // Return affected row count @Query("UPDATE users SET status = 'archived' WHERE created_at < :cutoff") suspend fun archiveOlderThan(cutoff: Long): Int }
Flow vs suspend in DAOs:
Flow<List<T>> — for observing data. Room re-emits when the table changes. Use for UI lists. Room runs the query on a background thread internally.
suspend fun — for one-shot reads/writes. Use for insert, update, delete, or single lookups. Caller is responsible for providing a coroutine context (typically Dispatchers.IO via the Repository).

Database, Builder, and TypeConverters

The @Database class is a singleton that holds the database connection. Always use the builder pattern to configure migrations, threading, and callbacks:

@Database( entities = [User::class, Post::class], version = 1, exportSchema = true // generates JSON schema for migration testing ) @TypeConverters(Converters::class) abstract class AppDatabase : RoomDatabase() { abstract fun userDao(): UserDao abstract fun postDao(): PostDao } // TypeConverters — store complex types in SQLite columns class Converters { @TypeConverter fun fromTimestamp(value: Long?): Date? = value?.let { Date(it) } @TypeConverter fun dateToTimestamp(date: Date?): Long? = date?.time // Use Gson/Moshi for complex objects (lists, maps) @TypeConverter fun fromStringList(value: String?): List<String> = value?.let { Json.decodeFromString(it) } ?: emptyList() @TypeConverter fun toStringList(list: List<String>): String = Json.encodeToString(list) // Enum converter @TypeConverter fun fromStatus(status: UserStatus): String = status.name @TypeConverter fun toStatus(value: String): UserStatus = UserStatus.valueOf(value) }
// Database builder with full configuration fun provideDatabase(context: Context): AppDatabase = Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db") .addMigrations(MIGRATION_1_2, MIGRATION_2_3) .addCallback(object : RoomDatabase.Callback() { override fun onCreate(db: SupportSQLiteDatabase) { super.onCreate(db) // Pre-populate with seed data on first create } }) .setJournalMode(RoomDatabase.JournalMode.WRITE_AHEAD_LOGGING) .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() // last resort: wipe DB if no migration path .build()
exportSchema = true is critical for testing. Room exports a JSON schema file for each version to your build directory. The MigrationTestHelper uses these files to verify migrations at every version step. Set room.schemaLocation in your build.gradle to control where schemas are saved.

Room Migrations — Auto vs Manual

When your schema changes between versions, you must provide a migration path. Without one, Room throws IllegalStateException (or destroys the DB if you used fallbackToDestructiveMigration()). Room offers two migration approaches:

Auto-Migration (Room 2.4+)

Room can auto-generate migrations for simple, unambiguous schema changes. It compares the exported JSON schemas of two versions and generates the SQL:

@Database( version = 3, entities = [User::class, Post::class], autoMigrations = [ AutoMigration(from = 1, to = 2), AutoMigration(from = 2, to = 3, spec = Migration2To3::class) ], exportSchema = true ) abstract class AppDatabase : RoomDatabase() { ... } // Spec needed for ambiguous changes (rename, delete) @RenameColumn(tableName = "users", fromColumnName = "name", toColumnName = "full_name") class Migration2To3 : AutoMigrationSpec
Auto-Migration Capabilities: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Change Type No Spec Needed Spec Required ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Add new column yes - Add new table yes - Add new index yes - Change column default yes - Rename column - @RenameColumn Rename table - @RenameTable Delete column - @DeleteColumn Delete table - @DeleteTable Change column type Not supported — use manual Complex data transform Not supported — use manual ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Key requirement: exportSchema = true must be set for auto-migrations. Room needs the JSON schema files of both the source and target versions to compute the diff. Without them, auto-migration cannot work.

Manual Migration

For complex changes that auto-migration cannot handle (column type changes, data transforms, merging tables), write a Migration object with raw SQL:

// Manual migration for complex data transform val MIGRATION_3_4 = object : Migration(3, 4) { override fun migrate(db: SupportSQLiteDatabase) { // Step 1: Create new table with desired schema db.execSQL(""" CREATE TABLE users_new ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL, full_name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '' ) """.trimIndent()) // Step 2: Copy + transform data from old table db.execSQL(""" INSERT INTO users_new (id, full_name, email) SELECT id, first_name || ' ' || last_name, email FROM users """.trimIndent()) // Step 3: Drop old, rename new db.execSQL("DROP TABLE users") db.execSQL("ALTER TABLE users_new RENAME TO users") } } // You can chain auto + manual migrations Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDatabase::class.java, "app.db") .addMigrations(MIGRATION_3_4) .build()

Testing Migrations

// Use MigrationTestHelper to verify every migration step @get:Rule val helper = MigrationTestHelper( InstrumentationRegistry.getInstrumentation(), AppDatabase::class.java ) @Test fun migrate3To4() { // Create DB at version 3 with test data helper.createDatabase(TEST_DB, 3).apply { execSQL("INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'John', 'Doe', 'john@test.com')") close() } // Run migration 3→4 and validate val db = helper.runMigrationsAndValidate(TEST_DB, 4, true, MIGRATION_3_4) val cursor = db.query("SELECT full_name FROM users WHERE id = 1") cursor.moveToFirst() assertEquals("John Doe", cursor.getString(0)) }
Always test migrations! Room provides MigrationTestHelper to verify migrations don't lose data. Without migrations, Room drops and recreates the database, losing all user data. Test every migration step (1→2, 2→3, etc.) and also the full chain (1→latest).

Room Indexing and Query Performance

Indexes are B-tree data structures that SQLite maintains alongside your table. They dramatically speed up read queries but impose a cost on writes:

@Entity( tableName = "orders", indices = [ Index("customerId"), // speeds up WHERE customerId = ? Index("status", "createdAt"), // composite: WHERE status = ? ORDER BY createdAt Index("orderNumber", unique = true), // unique constraint + index Index(value = ["customerId", "status"]) // covers multi-column queries ], foreignKeys = [ForeignKey( entity = Customer::class, parentColumns = ["id"], childColumns = ["customerId"], onDelete = ForeignKey.CASCADE )] ) data class Order( @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0, val customerId: Long, val orderNumber: String, val status: String, val createdAt: Long, val total: Double )
Indexing Rules of Thumb: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ DO index DON'T index ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Foreign key columns Boolean columns (low cardinality) Columns in WHERE clauses Columns rarely used in queries Columns in ORDER BY Tables with heavy write traffic Columns in JOIN conditions Very small tables (<100 rows) Unique constraints Columns updated frequently ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Composite index order matters! Index("status", "createdAt") helps: WHERE status = ? ORDER BY createdAt ✓ WHERE status = ? ✓ Does NOT help: WHERE createdAt = ? ✗ (wrong column order) ORDER BY createdAt ✗ (leftmost column missing) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Always index foreign key columns! Room warns at compile time if a foreign key column is not indexed. Without an index, DELETE CASCADE on the parent table triggers a full table scan of the child table.

WAL Mode and Concurrency

Room uses Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) by default (since Room 2.0). Understanding WAL is important for multi-threaded apps:

WAL vs Journal Mode: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Journal (legacy) WAL (default) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Concurrent reads Yes Yes Read during write BLOCKED (reader waits) ALLOWED (snapshot) Multiple writers No (serialized) No (serialized) Write performance Slower (copy-on-write) Faster (append-only) Disk usage Stable WAL file grows until checkpoint ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WAL writes changes to a log file first, then merges into the main database during checkpoints. This means readers see a consistent snapshot of the database even while a writer is appending new data. This is ideal for Android apps where coroutines often read and write concurrently from different threads.

DataStore — Replacing SharedPreferences

DataStore is a modern, Coroutines + Flow-based data storage solution that addresses all the shortcomings of SharedPreferences. It comes in two flavors:

Preferences DataStore vs Proto DataStore: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Preferences DS Proto DS ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Type safety Key-based (typed keys) Full (protobuf schema) Setup complexity Simple (no schema) Needs .proto file + codegen Schema evolution None (add keys freely) protobuf handles backward compat Validation Manual Schema-enforced at compile time Default values Per key, in code Defined in protobuf schema Best for Simple key-value Complex/structured settings (theme, locale, flags) (user profile, app config) Migration from SP Built-in helper Built-in helper Dependencies datastore-preferences datastore + protobuf plugin ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Preferences DataStore

// Define the DataStore instance (top-level, one per file) private val Context.dataStore by preferencesDataStore(name = "settings") class SettingsRepository(private val context: Context) { // Typed keys prevent get/put type mismatches private val THEME_KEY = stringPreferencesKey("theme") private val NOTIFICATIONS_KEY = booleanPreferencesKey("notifications") private val FONT_SIZE_KEY = intPreferencesKey("font_size") // Read: returns a Flow (reactive, never blocks) val theme: Flow<String> = context.dataStore.data .catch { e -> if (e is IOException) emit(emptyPreferences()) else throw e } .map { prefs -> prefs[THEME_KEY] ?: "system" } // Read multiple values as a data class val settings: Flow<AppSettings> = context.dataStore.data .catch { e -> if (e is IOException) emit(emptyPreferences()) else throw e } .map { prefs -> AppSettings( theme = prefs[THEME_KEY] ?: "system", notifications = prefs[NOTIFICATIONS_KEY] ?: true, fontSize = prefs[FONT_SIZE_KEY] ?: 14 ) } // Write: suspend function, atomic transaction suspend fun setTheme(theme: String) { context.dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[THEME_KEY] = theme } } // Atomic multi-key update suspend fun resetToDefaults() { context.dataStore.edit { prefs -> prefs[THEME_KEY] = "system" prefs[NOTIFICATIONS_KEY] = true prefs[FONT_SIZE_KEY] = 14 } } }

Proto DataStore

Proto DataStore provides full type safety with Protocol Buffers. You define your schema in a .proto file and the protobuf plugin generates the Kotlin class:

// 1. Define schema in app/src/main/proto/settings.proto syntax = "proto3"; option java_package = "com.example.app"; option java_multiple_files = true; message AppSettings { string theme = 1; bool notifications_enabled = 2; int32 font_size = 3; enum Language { ENGLISH = 0; SPANISH = 1; FRENCH = 2; } Language language = 4; } // 2. Create a Serializer object AppSettingsSerializer : Serializer<AppSettings> { override val defaultValue: AppSettings = AppSettings.getDefaultInstance() override suspend fun readFrom(input: InputStream): AppSettings = try { AppSettings.parseFrom(input) } catch (e: InvalidProtocolBufferException) { throw CorruptionException("Cannot read proto.", e) } override suspend fun writeTo(t: AppSettings, output: OutputStream) = t.writeTo(output) } // 3. Create the DataStore val Context.settingsDataStore by dataStore( fileName = "app_settings.pb", serializer = AppSettingsSerializer ) // 4. Read and write with full type safety val language: Flow<AppSettings.Language> = context.settingsDataStore.data .map { it.language } suspend fun setLanguage(lang: AppSettings.Language) { context.settingsDataStore.updateData { current -> current.toBuilder().setLanguage(lang).build() } }
When to choose Proto over Preferences: Use Proto DataStore when you have more than ~5 related settings, need schema evolution (protobuf handles adding/removing fields safely), or want compile-time guarantees that all settings are valid. Preferences DataStore is fine for 1-3 simple flags.

Migration from SharedPreferences

Both DataStore flavors provide built-in one-time migration from SharedPreferences. The migration runs on first DataStore access and then deletes the old SP file:

// Preferences DataStore migration private val Context.dataStore by preferencesDataStore( name = "settings", produceMigrations = { context -> listOf(SharedPreferencesMigration(context, "old_shared_prefs")) } ) // Keys in old SP are automatically mapped to DataStore keys // with matching names. After migration, old SP file is deleted. // Proto DataStore migration with key mapping val Context.settingsDataStore by dataStore( fileName = "app_settings.pb", serializer = AppSettingsSerializer, produceMigrations = { context -> listOf( SharedPreferencesMigration(context, "old_prefs") { sharedPrefs, currentData -> currentData.toBuilder() .setTheme(sharedPrefs.getString("theme", "system") ?: "system") .setNotificationsEnabled(sharedPrefs.getBoolean("notifications", true)) .build() } ) } )
SharedPreferences pitfalls that DataStore fixes:
1. getString() blocks the calling thread — can cause ANR on slow I/O
2. apply() can silently fail — no error notification to the caller
3. No transactional edits — concurrent writes from different threads can corrupt data
4. No type safety — calling getInt() on a key stored as String crashes at runtime
5. commit() blocks and apply() can lose writes on process death (pending writes are in-memory only)
6. No schema evolution — adding/removing keys is manual and error-prone

Scoped Storage (API 30+)

Starting with Android 11 (API 30), apps can no longer freely access the shared external storage filesystem. The MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permission is restricted to file managers and backup apps. All other apps must use scoped access:

Scoped Storage Access Model: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Storage Type API Permission ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ App-specific getFilesDir() None needed (internal) getCacheDir() Deleted on uninstall App-specific getExternalFilesDir() None needed (external) getExternalCacheDir() Deleted on uninstall MediaStore Images, video, audio READ_MEDIA_IMAGES/VIDEO (shared media) via MediaStore API /AUDIO (API 33+) or READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE SAF (Storage User picks files/dirs None needed (user Access Framework) via system file picker grants on selection) FileProvider Share app files with None (content:// URI other apps securely with temp permission) Downloads Via MediaStore or SAF Scoped: own files only ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

App-Specific Storage

No permissions needed. Files are private to your app and deleted on uninstall. Use for caches, databases, downloaded content the user does not need to keep:

// Internal storage — always available, private to app val internalFile = File(context.filesDir, "data.json") val cacheFile = File(context.cacheDir, "temp.dat") // External app-specific — more space, still private val externalFile = File(context.getExternalFilesDir(null), "export.csv") val externalCache = File(context.getExternalCacheDir(), "image_cache")

MediaStore API

MediaStore is the system database for shared media (photos, videos, audio, downloads). Your app can write its own files without permission but needs READ_MEDIA_* permissions to read other apps' media:

// Write an image to shared storage (no permission needed) val contentValues = ContentValues().apply { put(MediaStore.Images.Media.DISPLAY_NAME, "photo_${System.currentTimeMillis()}.jpg") put(MediaStore.Images.Media.MIME_TYPE, "image/jpeg") put(MediaStore.Images.Media.RELATIVE_PATH, "Pictures/MyApp") // IS_PENDING = 1 hides file from other apps while writing put(MediaStore.Images.Media.IS_PENDING, 1) } val uri = contentResolver.insert(MediaStore.Images.Media.EXTERNAL_CONTENT_URI, contentValues) uri?.let { contentResolver.openOutputStream(it)?.use { outputStream -> bitmap.compress(Bitmap.CompressFormat.JPEG, 90, outputStream) } // Mark as complete — now visible to other apps contentValues.clear() contentValues.put(MediaStore.Images.Media.IS_PENDING, 0) contentResolver.update(it, contentValues, null, null) } // Query images from shared storage (needs READ_MEDIA_IMAGES on API 33+) val projection = arrayOf( MediaStore.Images.Media._ID, MediaStore.Images.Media.DISPLAY_NAME, MediaStore.Images.Media.SIZE ) val selection = "${MediaStore.Images.Media.MIME_TYPE} = ?" val selectionArgs = arrayOf("image/jpeg") val sortOrder = "${MediaStore.Images.Media.DATE_ADDED} DESC" contentResolver.query( MediaStore.Images.Media.EXTERNAL_CONTENT_URI, projection, selection, selectionArgs, sortOrder )?.use { cursor -> val idCol = cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow(MediaStore.Images.Media._ID) val nameCol = cursor.getColumnIndexOrThrow(MediaStore.Images.Media.DISPLAY_NAME) while (cursor.moveToNext()) { val id = cursor.getLong(idCol) val name = cursor.getString(nameCol) val contentUri = ContentUris.withAppendedId( MediaStore.Images.Media.EXTERNAL_CONTENT_URI, id ) // Use contentUri to load with Coil/Glide } }

Storage Access Framework (SAF)

SAF lets the user pick files or directories through the system file picker. No permissions needed because the user explicitly grants access. Use for document editing, importing/exporting user files:

// Modern approach using Activity Result API val pickDocument = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.OpenDocument() ) { uri: Uri? -> uri?.let { readDocument(it) } } // Launch the picker pickDocument.launch(arrayOf("application/pdf", "text/plain")) // Create a new document val createDocument = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.CreateDocument("application/pdf") ) { uri: Uri? -> uri?.let { writeToDocument(it) } } // Pick a directory for persistent access val pickDirectory = registerForActivityResult( ActivityResultContracts.OpenDocumentTree() ) { uri: Uri? -> uri?.let { // Persist permission across reboots contentResolver.takePersistableUriPermission( it, Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION or Intent.FLAG_GRANT_WRITE_URI_PERMISSION ) } }

FileProvider — Sharing Files Securely

FileProvider generates content:// URIs for your app-private files so other apps can access them with temporary permission. Essential for share intents, camera captures, and email attachments:

// 1. Declare in AndroidManifest.xml <provider android:name="androidx.core.content.FileProvider" android:authorities="${applicationId}.provider" android:exported="false" android:grantUriPermissions="true"> <meta-data android:name="android.support.FILE_PROVIDER_PATHS" android:resource="@xml/file_paths" /> </provider> // 2. Define paths in res/xml/file_paths.xml <paths> <files-path name="internal" path="exports/" /> <cache-path name="cache" path="images/" /> <external-files-path name="external" path="shared/" /> </paths> // 3. Generate content:// URI and share val file = File(context.filesDir, "exports/report.pdf") val uri = FileProvider.getUriForFile(context, "${context.packageName}.provider", file) val shareIntent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND).apply { putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_STREAM, uri) type = "application/pdf" addFlags(Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION) } startActivity(Intent.createChooser(shareIntent, "Share report"))
Never share file:// URIs! Starting with API 24 (Android 7), sharing a file:// URI with another app throws FileUriExposedException. Always use FileProvider to generate content:// URIs. This also prevents exposing your app's internal file structure.

Content Providers

Content Providers expose data to other apps via a structured URI-based API. In modern Android, they are primarily needed for:

  • Cross-app data sharing — Contacts, Calendar, MediaStore all use Content Providers
  • CursorLoader integration — Legacy apps using LoaderManager
  • Search suggestions — Providing search results to the system
  • App widgets — Sharing data with RemoteViews
// ContentResolver: read contacts (needs READ_CONTACTS permission) val cursor = contentResolver.query( ContactsContract.Contacts.CONTENT_URI, arrayOf(ContactsContract.Contacts.DISPLAY_NAME), null, null, null ) cursor?.use { while (it.moveToNext()) { val name = it.getString(0) } }
Do you need a Content Provider? Probably not. If your data is app-internal, use Room directly. Content Providers add complexity (URI design, permissions, MIME types, cursor management). Only use them when you must expose data to other apps, provide search suggestions, or feed data to app widgets.

Caching Strategies and the Repository Pattern

The Repository pattern is the foundation of clean data architecture in Android. The Repository sits between your ViewModel and data sources (network, database, cache), providing a clean API and hiding implementation details:

Basic Offline-First Repository

// Repository pattern: Room (cache) + Network (source of truth) class ArticleRepository( private val localDao: ArticleDao, private val remoteApi: ArticleApi, private val ioDispatcher: CoroutineDispatcher = Dispatchers.IO ) { // UI observes local DB — always has data, even offline fun getArticles(): Flow<List<Article>> = localDao.observeAll() // Sync: fetch from network, save to cache suspend fun refresh() = withContext(ioDispatcher) { val remote = remoteApi.fetchAll() localDao.upsertAll(remote) } }

TTL-Based Cache Invalidation

Avoid unnecessary network requests by tracking when data was last fetched:

// TTL-based cache invalidation with status tracking class CachedArticleRepository( private val localDao: ArticleDao, private val remoteApi: ArticleApi, private val cacheTtlMs: Long = 15 * 60 * 1000 // 15 minutes ) { private var lastFetchTime = 0L fun getArticles(): Flow<List<Article>> = localDao.observeAll() .onStart { val now = System.currentTimeMillis() if (now - lastFetchTime > cacheTtlMs) { try { val remote = remoteApi.fetchAll() localDao.upsertAll(remote) lastFetchTime = now } catch (e: IOException) { // Offline — serve stale cache, don't crash } } } }

Repository with Loading State

Expose loading/error state alongside data so the UI can show progress indicators and error messages:

// Sealed class for representing data + loading state sealed class Resource<out T> { data class Success<T>(val data: T) : Resource<T>() data class Error(val message: String, val cause: Throwable? = null) : Resource<Nothing>() object Loading : Resource<Nothing>() } class ArticleRepository( private val localDao: ArticleDao, private val remoteApi: ArticleApi ) { fun getArticles(): Flow<Resource<List<Article>>> = flow { // 1. Emit cached data immediately val cached = localDao.getAll() if (cached.isNotEmpty()) emit(Resource.Success(cached)) // 2. Show loading while fetching emit(Resource.Loading) // 3. Fetch fresh data try { val remote = remoteApi.fetchAll() localDao.upsertAll(remote) // 4. Emit updated data from DB (single source of truth) emit(Resource.Success(localDao.getAll())) } catch (e: IOException) { if (cached.isNotEmpty()) { emit(Resource.Success(cached)) // stale but usable } else { emit(Resource.Error("No internet and no cached data")) } } } }
Offline-First Architecture: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ UI (Compose) ←── ViewModel ←── Repository │ │ Room DB Network (cache) (source) │ Single Source of Truth Flow: 1. UI observes Room via Flow 2. Repository fetches from network in background 3. Network data saved to Room 4. Room Flow auto-emits update to UI 5. If network fails, cached data is still available ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Caching Strategies: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Cache-first Show cache → fetch in background → update Network-first Fetch → fall back to cache on failure Stale-while- Show stale cache immediately, refresh behind revalidate the scenes, emit fresh data when ready TTL-based Only fetch if cache is older than threshold Forced refresh Pull-to-refresh ignores cache, always fetches ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Conflict resolution strategies: When local and remote both change the same record:
Last-write-wins — simple timestamp comparison, may lose edits
Server-wins — safe default for most apps, server is authoritative
Client-wins — preserves offline edits, risks overwriting server changes
Merge — field-level merge preserving both changes (complex but robust)
Queue-based — queue local changes, replay against server state on sync

Using WorkManager for Reliable Sync

For sync operations that must complete even if the app is killed, combine the Repository with WorkManager:

// Periodic sync with WorkManager class SyncWorker( context: Context, params: WorkerParameters, private val repository: ArticleRepository ) : CoroutineWorker(context, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { return try { repository.refresh() Result.success() } catch (e: IOException) { Result.retry() // will retry with exponential backoff } } } // Schedule periodic sync val syncRequest = PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>( repeatInterval = 1, repeatIntervalTimeUnit = TimeUnit.HOURS ) .setConstraints(Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) .build()) .build() WorkManager.getInstance(context) .enqueueUniquePeriodicWork("sync", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP, syncRequest)

questions.title: Room DAO
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a Room DAO.
questions.explanation: @Dao marks the interface. @Query allows custom SQL with :paramName binding. @Insert and @Delete handle common operations. onConflict = REPLACE creates an upsert.
hints: Room uses annotations to generate SQL boilerplate

questions.title: Room on Main Thread
questions.description: This code crashes: "Cannot access database on the main thread." Fix it.

checks.message: Use Dispatchers.IO or collect a Flow for database operations

questions.explanation: Room throws if you access the database on the main thread. Use a Flow return type in the DAO — Room runs the query on a background thread and emits updates reactively. collectAsState() converts it to Compose state.
hints: Room can return Flow<List<T>> for reactive queries

questions.title: DataStore vs SharedPreferences
questions.description: Why does Google recommend DataStore over SharedPreferences?
options: DataStore is faster for large datasets
DataStore is async, type-safe (Proto), handles errors, and does not block the UI thread
DataStore encrypts data by default
DataStore supports SQL queries
questions.explanation: SharedPreferences blocks the calling thread, can fail silently, and has no type safety. DataStore is fully async via Coroutines/Flow, has proper error handling, and Proto DataStore adds type safety with Protocol Buffers.
hints: What happens when you call SharedPreferences.getString() on the main thread?

questions.title: Room Migration
questions.description: What does this Room migration do?
options: Renames the users table to version 3
Adds an email column and index when upgrading from DB version 2 to 3
Creates a completely new users table
Backs up the database
questions.explanation: Migration(2, 3) runs when upgrading from version 2 to 3. It adds a non-null email column with a default value (so existing rows are valid) and creates an index for faster queries. Without migrations, Room would drop and recreate the database, losing all data.
hints: The two numbers represent source and target versions

questions.title: Preferences DataStore
questions.description: Write a DataStore-backed class that stores and retrieves a user's preferred theme (dark/light) as a string.

checks.message: Expose a Flow for reactive reading
Use suspend edit function for writing

questions.explanation: preferencesDataStore creates a DataStore delegate. stringPreferencesKey creates a typed key. Reading returns a Flow that emits on changes. Writing uses edit { } which is a suspend function handling transactions atomically.
hints: DataStore uses typed keys: stringPreferencesKey, intPreferencesKey, etc.
Reading is a Flow, writing is a suspend function

questions.title: Room Relationship
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a one-to-many relationship between User and their Posts in Room.

blanks.hint: Includes all columns of the nested entity

questions.explanation: @PrimaryKey marks primary key fields. @Embedded includes the parent entity's columns. @Relation defines how child entities relate via parentColumn and entityColumn. Room automatically joins the tables when querying UserWithPosts.
hints: Room uses annotations to define relationships between entities
The relationship connects a parent column to a child column

questions.title: Room Auto-Migration
questions.description: What does this auto-migration configuration do?
options: It creates the database from scratch with version 3
It defines two auto-migrations: v1→v2 is fully automatic, v2→v3 renames the 'name' column to 'full_name' using a migration spec
It deletes all data when upgrading from v1 to v3
It only works on new installations, not upgrades
questions.explanation: Room auto-migrations handle simple schema changes automatically. The v1→v2 migration needs no spec (probably just adds a column or table). The v2→v3 migration uses @RenameColumn in a spec class because Room can't guess that "name" became "full_name" (it looks like a delete + add). Without the spec, Room would drop the old column and add a new empty one, losing data.
hints: Renames are ambiguous — Room sees a column disappear and a new one appear
The spec resolves ambiguity by telling Room it's the same column with a new name

questions.title: Room Indexing
questions.description: When should you add an @Index to a Room entity column?
options: Always — indices make everything faster
When you frequently query or sort by that column — indices speed up reads but slow down writes because the index must be maintained
Only for primary key columns
Never — Room automatically indexes everything
questions.explanation: Indices are a read/write tradeoff. They create a sorted data structure that speeds up WHERE clauses and ORDER BY on the indexed column(s). But every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update the index, slowing writes. Add indices for columns used in: frequent queries, JOIN conditions, foreign keys, and ORDER BY. Don't index columns that are rarely queried or on tables with heavy write traffic.
hints: Indices are like a book's index — fast lookups, but costs space and maintenance

questions.title: WAL Mode
questions.description: What advantage does WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) provide in Room, and why is it the default?
options: WAL encrypts all database writes for security
WAL allows concurrent reads and writes — readers don't block writers, improving performance in multi-threaded apps
WAL compresses the database file to save storage
WAL prevents data loss by writing to two databases simultaneously
questions.explanation: WAL mode writes changes to a separate log file instead of modifying the database directly. This means readers see a consistent snapshot while writers append to the log — no locking conflicts. Without WAL (journal mode), writes lock the entire database, blocking all readers. WAL is ideal for Android where coroutines may read and write concurrently. Checkpoints periodically merge the WAL file into the main database.
hints: Think about what happens when one coroutine reads while another writes

questions.title: Offline-First Repository
questions.description: Write a repository that follows the offline-first pattern: always read from the local database, sync from network in the background, and expose data as a Flow.

checks.message: Return a Flow for reactive updates
Save remote data to local DB

questions.explanation: The offline-first pattern: (1) UI observes the local database via Flow — always has data to show, even offline. (2) refresh() fetches from the network and upserts into the local DB. (3) Because the UI observes the DB, it automatically updates when new data is inserted. The local DB is the single source of truth — the UI never reads from the network directly. This pattern is recommended by Google's architecture guide.
hints: The UI should never read from the network directly
Room Flow queries automatically emit when data changes
